an3.62:1.1: "These three are fears that separate mother from son, *bhikkhus* — so says the uninstructed ordinary person.
an3.62:1.3: There comes a time, *bhikkhus*, when a great conflagration arises.
an3.62:1.4: When a great conflagration has arisen, *bhikkhus*, villages are burned by it, towns are burned, cities are burned.
an3.62:1.5: When villages are burning, towns are burning, cities are burning, there a mother cannot find her son, nor a son his mother.
an3.62:1.6: This, *bhikkhus*, the uninstructed ordinary person calls the first fear that separates mother from son.
an3.62:10.1: There is, *bhikkhus*, a path, a way, that leads to the abandoning and transcending of these three fears that do not separate mother from son and these three fears that truly separate mother from son.
an3.62:10.2: And what, *bhikkhus*, is the path, what is the way that leads to the abandoning and transcending of these three fears that do not separate mother from son and these three fears that truly separate mother from son?
an3.62:10.3: It is this very noble eightfold path, namely—
an3.62:10.4: right view, right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right *sati*, right *samādhi*.
an3.62:10.5: This, *bhikkhus*, is the path, this is the way that leads to the abandoning and transcending of these three fears that do not separate mother from son and these three fears that truly separate mother from son."
an3.62:2.1: Again, *bhikkhus*, there comes a time when a great storm cloud arises.
an3.62:2.2: When a great storm cloud has arisen, *bhikkhus*, a great floodwater comes forth.
an3.62:2.3: When a great floodwater has come forth, *bhikkhus*, villages are swept away by it, towns are swept away, cities are swept away.
an3.62:2.4: When villages are being swept away, towns are being swept away, cities are being swept away, there a mother cannot find her son, nor a son his mother.
an3.62:2.5: This, *bhikkhus*, the uninstructed ordinary person calls the second fear that separates mother from son.
an3.62:3.1: Again, *bhikkhus*, there comes a time when there is danger, forest upheaval, and the people of the countryside mount their wagons and flee.
an3.62:3.2: When there is danger, *bhikkhus*, forest upheaval, and the people of the countryside have mounted their wagons and are fleeing, there a mother cannot find her son, nor a son his mother.
an3.62:3.3: This, *bhikkhus*, the uninstructed ordinary person calls the third fear that separates mother from son.
an3.62:3.4: These, *bhikkhus*, are the three fears that separate mother from son — so says the uninstructed ordinary person.
an3.62:4.1: But these three, *bhikkhus* — these very three fears that do not separate mother from son — the uninstructed ordinary person calls fears that separate mother from son.
an3.62:4.3: There comes a time, *bhikkhus*, when a great conflagration arises.
an3.62:4.4: When a great conflagration has arisen, *bhikkhus*, villages are burned by it, towns are burned, cities are burned.
an3.62:4.5: When villages are burning, towns are burning, cities are burning, there comes a time when occasionally a mother does find her son, and a son his mother.
an3.62:4.6: This, *bhikkhus*, the uninstructed ordinary person calls the first fear that separates mother from son — but it is a fear that does not separate mother from son.
an3.62:5.1: Again, *bhikkhus*, there comes a time when a great storm cloud arises.
an3.62:5.2: When a great storm cloud has arisen, *bhikkhus*, a great floodwater comes forth.
an3.62:5.3: When a great floodwater has come forth, *bhikkhus*, villages are swept away by it, towns are swept away, cities are swept away.
an3.62:5.4: When villages are being swept away, towns are being swept away, cities are being swept away, there comes a time when occasionally a mother does find her son, and a son his mother.
an3.62:5.5: This, *bhikkhus*, the uninstructed ordinary person calls the second fear that separates mother from son — but it is a fear that does not separate mother from son.
an3.62:6.1: Again, *bhikkhus*, there comes a time when there is danger, forest upheaval, and the people of the countryside mount their wagons and flee.
an3.62:6.2: When there is danger, *bhikkhus*, forest upheaval, and the people of the countryside have mounted their wagons and are fleeing, there comes a time when occasionally a mother does find her son, and a son his mother.
an3.62:6.3: This, *bhikkhus*, the uninstructed ordinary person calls the third fear that separates mother from son — but it is a fear that does not separate mother from son.
an3.62:6.4: These, *bhikkhus*, are the three fears that do not separate mother from son — which the uninstructed ordinary person calls fears that separate mother from son.
an3.62:7.1: These, *bhikkhus*, are the three fears that truly separate mother from son.
an3.62:7.3: The fear of aging, the fear of illness, and the fear of death.
an3.62:7.4: A mother, *bhikkhus*, cannot obtain this for her aging son:
an3.62:7.5: 'May I age instead; may my son not age.'
an3.62:7.6: Nor can a son obtain this for his aging mother:
an3.62:7.7: 'May I age instead; may my mother not age.'
an3.62:8.1: A mother, *bhikkhus*, cannot obtain this for her ailing son:
an3.62:8.2: 'May I fall ill instead; may my son not fall ill.'
an3.62:8.3: Nor can a son obtain this for his ailing mother:
an3.62:8.4: 'May I fall ill instead; may my mother not fall ill.'
an3.62:9.1: A mother, *bhikkhus*, cannot obtain this for her dying son:
an3.62:9.2: 'May I die instead; may my son not die.'
an3.62:9.3: Nor can a son obtain this for his dying mother:
an3.62:9.4: 'May I die instead; may my mother not die.'
an3.62:9.5: These, *bhikkhus*, are the three fears that truly separate mother from son.
